@aaronpk This looks really awesome, thank you for laying all this groundwork. People like you make me hopeful in a time when hope is rare!
since I deleted Facebook and Instagram I no longer see weird ads tailored to things I've only even spoken out loud so that's pretty cool

@micahstubbs I deleted my Facebook a few months ago and there a photos and videos from friends hidden in there that aren’t accessible any other way. I think that is a profoundly cynical and disturbing state of affairs considering the intent of the technology it is built on top of.

@micahstubbs A “healthy” use of the web would be opening a browser to the latest updates from artists personal sites, distributed and owned by them, curated by an algorithm controlled and understood by me. I want to see a future like that, but we collectively cede control every day.

@micahstubbs I mean, I am old enough to have “gone viral” by being posted on a bunch of blogs consumed through Google Reader. Obviously that couldn’t happen today- but I think the reasons why are a conscious decision by platforms to take more control of the distribution cuz it benefits them.

@micahstubbs I mean, how does HTTP exist? How does D3 exist? Who wrote the specs for RSS, and why? – The foundations were often created without ads & data funding in mind, and were created nonetheless because they were needed.

Maybe the problem is we have been treating our personal websites as output only services. But really, the best websites are two-way. I think we should all be building our own feeds, making our own "algorithms" that we control and understand.

This isn't even anti-facebook, I am truly saddened by how much energy has been spent by incredibly intelligent people to perform these cynical and dishonest scams. Imagine if engineers would work on interesting problems besides selling ads and personal data?

There is no reason that your personal website couldn't ping my personal website when you post an awesome new photo, and yet the entirety of some artists' work is held in an Instagram profile- these are choices we have made as developers, creators, and as a society

To my original point- if I was re-making a social network, it would mostly look like the internet at large, but with the tools of creation and hosting made easier and accessible. Truly accessible, not "gonna make some money off being the middleman" accessible.

The world would be dramatically better if we removed the tech-bro mold that is growing on top of our open protocols, re-selling convenience to users and stripping them of their autonomy and individuality and re-selling their data at every opportunity

The protocols already exist- and the existence of social media in our society reflects a profound failure of people making tools to make websites. I am lucky to own https://t.co/V0RPi2pMUj - how crazy is it that it's standard to host your identity under a corporate TLD?

Twitter was like this for a while- more a protocol than a social network- super easy API, a variety of clients & creators. Then they decided to make money of selling data access and driving up engagement with addictive notifications that aren't available through 3rd parties.
If I could switch all of my Facebook contacts over to a giant mailing list I would do that in a heartbeat- but there are reasons why others wouldn't (even though those reasons are blurry and varied)

If I was going to remake a social network in 2019 to be sustainable and not grow into an anti-user beast, I would make it open-source, super remixable and rebuildable, and based on the simplest and most resilient parts of the web
@trjedrey Isn't that much more distressing though, in an age of drones, that Americans don't care who dies as long as it's not them?
